subject: Science

unit.unitOverview: Assalaamu alaykum, young scientist! In this unit you are going to explore one of the biggest and most exciting questions in all of science: why do we have day and night? Every single morning the sky grows bright over the Indian Ocean, and every evening it turns dark and the stars come out over the savanna. You have watched this happen your whole life. Now you will finally discover the wonderful secret behind it.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, experiments and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured directly from the Stage 2 science workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: Explain that Earth spins (rotates) on its axis, and that this spinning gives us day and night.
Describe how the Sun appears to move across the sky from east to west, and explain why it only *appears* to move.
Tell the story of a day: sunrise in the east, the high midday sun, and sunset in the west.
Observe and describe how shadows change in length and direction through the day.
Explain that a shadow forms when an object blocks the light of the Sun.
Understand that when it is day where you live, it is night on the other side of Earth.
Explain that the Sun is a star - a huge ball of hot, glowing gas.
Use the Sun and shadows to help you find direction and guess the time.
Stay safe by never looking straight at the Sun.

concepts.title: The Earth and the Sun — part 1
concepts.explanation: Assalaamu alaykum, young scientist! In this unit you are going to explore one of the biggest and most exciting questions in all of science: why do we have day and night? Every single morning the sky grows bright over the Indian Ocean, and every evening it turns dark and the stars come out over the savanna. You have watched this happen your whole life. Now you will finally discover the wonderful secret behind it.
concepts.example: Think about your own day. When the sun rises over Mombasa or Kismaayo, the fishermen push their boats onto the water, the roosters crow, and your family wakes for the Fajr prayer. By the middle of the day the sun is…

concepts.title: The Earth and the Sun — part 2
concepts.explanation: Think about your own day. When the sun rises over Mombasa or Kismaayo, the fishermen push their boats onto the water, the roosters crow, and your family wakes for the Fajr prayer. By the middle of the day the sun is high and strong, the ground is hot under your feet, and the shadows grow small. In the evening the sun sinks toward the west, the air cools, and the camels and goats are brought home. All of these things follow a pattern that repeats again and again. In this unit you will learn that this pattern is…
concepts.example: You will learn why the sun looks like it moves across the sky, even though it is really you who is moving. You will learn how shadows can tell you the time of day, just as they helped people long before there were any…

concepts.title: The Earth and the Sun — part 3
concepts.explanation: You will learn why the sun looks like it moves across the sky, even though it is really you who is moving. You will learn how shadows can tell you the time of day, just as they helped people long before there were any clocks or phones. You will find out that the sun is really a star - the closest star to us - and that it gives our world the light and warmth that every plant, animal, and person needs to live. Learning about the sky is one way we can wonder at the beautiful world that has been created for us, and…
concepts.example: This is your lesson book. Read it slowly, a little each day. Whenever you see a box that says Ask Your AI Tutor, stop and ask your tutor that question out loud - your tutor is ready to help you day or night. Whenever…

concepts.title: The Earth and the Sun — part 4
concepts.explanation: This is your lesson book. Read it slowly, a little each day. Whenever you see a box that says Ask Your AI Tutor, stop and ask your tutor that question out loud - your tutor is ready to help you day or night. Whenever you see a Did You Know? box, get ready to be amazed. Let us begin our journey to the Earth and the Sun!
concepts.example: By the time you finish this unit, you will be able to do all of these things. Read them now, and read them again at the end to see how much you have grown as a scientist.

workedExamples.title: Reading Shadows and the Sun
workedExamples.prompt: Sometimes in science we measure things and work out an answer step by step. Let us do two examples together. Read each step slowly and make sure you understand it before moving on.
workedExamples.solution: Worked Example 1: How long is the shadow? Hassan puts a stick in the ground in the morning and measures its shadow with a ruler. Then he measures it again at midday and again in the late afternoon. Here is what he found: Time of Day Where the Sun Is

workedExamples.title: 1: How long is the shadow?
workedExamples.prompt: Hassan puts a stick in the ground in the morning and measures its shadow with a ruler. Then he measures it again at midday and again in the late afternoon. Here is what he found:
workedExamples.solution: Time of Day Where the Sun Is Shadow Length Early morning (about 7 o'clock)

workedExamples.title: 2: Using shadows to guess the time
workedExamples.prompt: Khadija is playing outside with no clock nearby. She looks at the shadow of the tall water tank. The shadow is very short, almost hiding under the tank, and the ground is very hot. What time of day is it, and in which direction should she look to find shade…
workedExamples.solution: Step 1: A very short shadow means the Sun is high in the sky. Step 2: The Sun is highest at midday, around noon. So it is about the middle of the day. Step 3: After midday the Sun moves toward the west, so shadows will start to grow and point toward the east. Khadija can expect longer, cooler shadows on the east side of buildings as the afternoon goes on. She decides to rest in the shade and drink some water until the strong midday heat passes. Clever thinking, Khadija! Reading Time: Yusuf and the Sunrise Read…

workedExamples.title: Guided example 4
workedExamples.prompt: In which direction does the Sun set? (a) East (b) West (c) North (d) Up
workedExamples.solution: (b) West. The Sun always appears to set in the west.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 5
workedExamples.prompt: When is the Sun highest in the sky? (a) Early morning (b) Midday (c) Evening (d) Midnight
workedExamples.solution: (b) Midday. The Sun is highest around noon, in the middle of the day.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 6
workedExamples.prompt: When the Sun is high in the sky, shadows are: (a) Very long (b) Very short (c) Gone forever (d) Blue
workedExamples.solution: (b) Very short. A high Sun makes short shadows; a low Sun makes long shadows.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 7
workedExamples.prompt: Which way does a shadow point? (a) Toward the Sun (b) Away from the Sun (c) Always north (d) Straight up
workedExamples.solution: (b) Away from the Sun. Light is blocked by the object, so the shadow falls on the far side.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 8
workedExamples.prompt: The Sun is really a: (a) Planet (b) Moon (c) Star (d) Cloud
workedExamples.solution: (c) Star. The Sun is a star - the closest one to Earth.

practice.prompt: What causes day and night? (a) The Sun turns off at night (b) Earth spins (c) Clouds cover the Sun (d) The Moon blocks the Sun
practice.answer: (b) Earth spins. Day and night are caused by Earth turning, not by the Sun switching off.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: How long does Earth take to spin around one time? (a) One hour (b) One week (c) One day (24 hours) (d) One year
practice.answer: (c) One day (24 hours). Earth makes one full spin every 24 hours.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: In which direction does the Sun rise? (a) North (b) South (c) East (d) West
practice.answer: (c) East. The Sun always appears to rise in the east.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: In which direction does the Sun set? (a) East (b) West (c) North (d) Up
practice.answer: (b) West. The Sun always appears to set in the west.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: When is the Sun highest in the sky? (a) Early morning (b) Midday (c) Evening (d) Midnight
practice.answer: (b) Midday. The Sun is highest around noon, in the middle of the day.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: When the Sun is high in the sky, shadows are: (a) Very long (b) Very short (c) Gone forever (d) Blue
practice.answer: (b) Very short. A high Sun makes short shadows; a low Sun makes long shadows.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Which way does a shadow point? (a) Toward the Sun (b) Away from the Sun (c) Always north (d) Straight up
practice.answer: (b) Away from the Sun. Light is blocked by the object, so the shadow falls on the far side.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: The Sun is really a: (a) Planet (b) Moon (c) Star (d) Cloud
practice.answer: (c) Star. The Sun is a star - the closest one to Earth.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: When it is daytime where you live, the opposite side of Earth has: (a) Day too (b) Night (c) No time (d) Rain
practice.answer: (b) Night. Only half of Earth faces the Sun at once, so the far side is dark.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: To make a shadow you need a light source, a surface, and: (a) Water (b) An object to block the light (c) A clock (d) Wind
practice.answer: (b) An object to block the light. Light, an object, and a surface are the three things needed.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Why does the Sun seem to move across the sky? (a) The Sun walks (b) Earth is spinning (c) The wind pushes it (d) It is pulled by the Moon
practice.answer: (b) Earth is spinning. The Sun only appears to move because we are turning with Earth.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: What should you NEVER do when studying the Sun? (a) Look at shadows (b) Wear a hat (c) Look straight at the Sun (d) Drink water
practice.answer: (c) Look straight at the Sun. This can badly hurt your eyes - always use shadows instead.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

activities.title: Observe: The Earth and the Sun — part 5
activities.materials: Notebook, pencil and safe things you can find at home
steps: Look for an example of the earth and the sun — part 5 around your home or outside.
Watch it closely for a few minutes and note three things you notice.
Write or draw what you observed, using the unit's science words.
Explain your finding to a family member or your teacher.

activities.title: Sort: The Earth and the Sun — part 6
activities.materials: Notebook, pencil and safe things you can find at home
steps: Look for an example of the earth and the sun — part 6 around your home or outside.
Find examples at home and sort them into groups, then explain your rule.
Write or draw what you observed, using the unit's science words.
Explain your finding to a family member or your teacher.

rules.title: Key idea 1
rules.text: The three most important ideas: 1) Earth spins once a day, and that gives us day and night. 2) The Sun only seems to move because we are moving. 3) Never, ever look straight at the Sun - use shadows to know where it is.

terms: The round planet we live on; it spins once a day and orbits the Sun once a year.
The star at the centre of our solar system that gives us light and heat.
To turn around and around like a top.
The invisible line Earth spins around.
Time when your part of Earth faces the Sun (light).
Time when your part of Earth faces away from the Sun (dark).
When the Sun appears in the east.
When the Sun sinks in the west.
A dark shape made when an object blocks light.
A huge ball of hot glowing gas that makes its own light.
A time of year with its own weather (rainy or dry).

vocabulary.meaning: The round planet we live on; it spins once a day and orbits the Sun once a year.
vocabulary.example: In this unit you will learn that this pattern is caused by our giant planet Earth spinning like a top.

vocabulary.meaning: The star at the centre of our solar system that gives us light and heat.
vocabulary.example: When the sun rises over Mombasa or Kismaayo, the fishermen push their boats onto the water, the roosters crow, and your family wakes for the Fajr prayer.

vocabulary.meaning: To turn around and around like a top.
The invisible line Earth spins around.
vocabulary.example: Explain that Earth spins (rotates) on its axis, and that this spinning gives us day and night.

vocabulary.meaning: Time when your part of Earth faces the Sun (light).
vocabulary.example: By the middle of the day the sun is high and strong, the ground is hot under your feet, and the shadows grow small.

vocabulary.meaning: Time when your part of Earth faces away from the Sun (dark).
vocabulary.example: Whenever you see a box that says Ask Your AI Tutor, stop and ask your tutor that question out loud - your tutor is ready to help you day or night.

vocabulary.meaning: When the Sun appears in the east.
vocabulary.example: Sunrise over the Indian Ocean is beautiful and pink.

vocabulary.meaning: When the Sun sinks in the west.
vocabulary.example: After sunset the air in the desert cools quickly.

vocabulary.meaning: A dark shape made when an object blocks light.
vocabulary.example: Explain that a shadow forms when an object blocks the light of the Sun.

vocabulary.meaning: A huge ball of hot glowing gas that makes its own light.
vocabulary.example: You will find out that the sun is really a star - the closest star to us - and that it gives our world the light and warmth that every plant, animal, and person needs to live.

vocabulary.meaning: A time of year with its own weather (rainy or dry).
vocabulary.example: A time of year with its own weather, like the dry season or the rainy season.
Describe how the Sun appears to move across the sky from east to west, and explain why it only *appears* to move.

commonMistakes: Saying the Sun moves around Earth each day.
It looks that way from the ground.
Thinking the Sun turns off at night.
We cannot see it, so it seems gone.
Thinking it is night everywhere at once.
It is night where we are.
Thinking the Sun is not a star.
It looks bigger and brighter than night stars.
A shadow looks solid and still.

connections.text: You already learned that light travels in straight lines and shadows form when light is blocked. This unit uses that to explain shadows made by the Sun.
Moon and Planets (future units): The words *orbit*, *star*, and *planet* will help you when you explore the Moon, the planets, and the whole solar system.
Directions (east, west, north, south) and time zones connect science to maps and the wider world.
The Sun's position marks the times of the daily prayers, and the crescent Moon marks the start of months like Ramadan.
Measuring shadow lengths and comparing them (longer, shorter) uses your measuring and comparing skills.
If you loved this unit, here are some ways to keep exploring:

fluency.answer: (b) Earth spins. Day and night are caused by Earth turning, not by the Sun switching off.

realProblems.prompt: Use your science to answer these questions about real life in East Africa.
realProblems.answer: A cooler, better time is the early morning or the late afternoon/evening, when the Sun is low, shadows are long, and there is more shade and cooler air. At midday the Sun is high and strong, so it is very hot.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: A cooler, better time is the early morning or the late afternoon/evening, when the Sun is low, shadows are long, and there is more shade and cooler air. At midday the Sun is high and strong, so it is very hot.

realProblems.prompt: Farah wants to play football with his friends but not in the strong midday heat. Using what you know about the Sun and shadows, when would be a cooler, better time to play - and why?
realProblems.answer: The strong midday sun gives lots of heat and light, which dries the water out of the mango slices quickly. A high, strong Sun is powerful, so it dries food fast.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: The strong midday sun gives lots of heat and light, which dries the water out of the mango slices quickly. A high, strong Sun is powerful, so it dries food fast.

realProblems.prompt: Amina's grandmother wants to dry sliced mango in the Sun. Why is the strong midday sun useful for drying food?
realProblems.answer: He is looking toward the east, because the Sun rises in the east. Once he knows east, he knows west is behind him, and he can work out north and south too.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: He is looking toward the east, because the Sun rises in the east. Once he knows east, he knows west is behind him, and he can work out north and south too.

realProblems.prompt: A fisherman near Kismaayo watches the sky lighten over the ocean. In which direction is he looking, and how does the sunrise help him know his directions?
realProblems.answer: Solar panels make the most electricity when the Sun's light is strongest and most direct, which is around midday on a clear day when the Sun is high in the sky.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Solar panels make the most electricity when the Sun's light is strongest and most direct, which is around midday on a clear day when the Sun is high in the sky.

realProblems.prompt: Omar's family has solar panels on their roof to make electricity. Why do the panels make the most electricity in the middle of a clear, sunny day?
realProblems.answer: During the day the Sun heats the ground. At night your part of Earth is turned away from the Sun, so no new warmth comes, and the heat escapes into the sky, so it cools down.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: During the day the Sun heats the ground. At night your part of Earth is turned away from the Sun, so no new warmth comes, and the heat escapes into the sky, so it cools down.

realProblems.prompt: During the dry season the days are hot but the nights are cool. Explain to a younger child why the night gets cool after the Sun goes down.
realProblems.answer: Work through the task and check with your teacher or the reference card.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Work through the task and check with your teacher or the reference card.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: In your own words, explain why we have day and night.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: We have day and night because Earth spins. When your part of Earth faces the Sun it is day; when it faces away it is night.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Is the Sun really moving across the sky? Explain what is really happening.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: No, the Sun is not really moving across the sky. Earth is spinning, which makes the Sun appear to move.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Describe how a shadow changes from the morning to the middle of the day.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: In the morning the shadow is long (the Sun is low). By midday the shadow becomes short (the Sun is high overhead).

reasoningPrompts.prompt: What three things do you need to make a shadow?
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: You need a light source (the Sun), an object to block the light, and a surface for the shadow to fall on.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Why is it night on the other side of the world when it is day where you live?
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Because Earth is a ball, the Sun can only light half of it at once. The half turned toward the Sun has day; the half turned away has night.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Why do we say the Sun is a star?
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: The Sun is a star because it is a huge ball of hot, glowing gas that makes its own light. It looks bigger and brighter only because it is the closest star to us.

questions.question: What causes day and night?
options: The Sun turns off at night
questions.hint: Use the Unit 6 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: Earth spins. Day and night are caused by Earth turning, not by the Sun switching off.

questions.question: How long does Earth take to spin around one time?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 6 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: One day (24 hours). Earth makes one full spin every 24 hours.

questions.question: In which direction does the Sun rise?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 6 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: East. The Sun always appears to rise in the east.

questions.question: In which direction does the Sun set?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 6 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: West. The Sun always appears to set in the west.

questions.question: When is the Sun highest in the sky?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 6 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: Midday. The Sun is highest around noon, in the middle of the day.

questions.question: When the Sun is high in the sky, shadows are:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 6 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: Very short. A high Sun makes short shadows; a low Sun makes long shadows.

questions.question: Which way does a shadow point?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 6 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: Away from the Sun. Light is blocked by the object, so the shadow falls on the far side.

questions.question: The Sun is really a:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 6 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: Star. The Sun is a star - the closest one to Earth.

questions.question: When it is daytime where you live, the opposite side of Earth has:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 6 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: Night. Only half of Earth faces the Sun at once, so the far side is dark.

questions.question: To make a shadow you need a light source, a surface, and:
options: An object to block the light
questions.answer: An object to block the light
questions.hint: Use the Unit 6 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: An object to block the light. Light, an object, and a surface are the three things needed.

questions.question: Why does the Sun seem to move across the sky?
options: It is pulled by the Moon
questions.hint: Use the Unit 6 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: Earth is spinning. The Sun only appears to move because we are turning with Earth.

questions.question: What should you NEVER do when studying the Sun?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 6 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: Look straight at the Sun. This can badly hurt your eyes - always use shadows instead.

selfAssessment: I can explain that Earth spins (rotates) on its axis, and that this spinning gives us day and night.
I can describe how the Sun appears to move across the sky from east to west, and explain why it only *appears* to move.
I can tell the story of a day: sunrise in the east, the high midday sun, and sunset in the west.
I can observe and describe how shadows change in length and direction through the day.
I can explain that a shadow forms when an object blocks the light of the Sun.
I can understand that when it is day where you live, it is night on the other side of Earth.
I can explain that the Sun is a star - a huge ball of hot, glowing gas.
I can use the Sun and shadows to help you find direction and guess the time.
